content.text: What Is Graphiti?

Graphiti is an open-source library (from the Zep team) that builds a temporal knowledge graph from unstructured text. Unlike a plain vector store, Graphiti extracts entities and relationships from each piece of text (called an episode), stores them as typed graph nodes and edges in Neo4j, and automatically resolves contradictions when new facts supersede old ones. Each edge carries valid_at and invalid_at timestamps, so the graph captures not just what is true but when it became true and when it was superseded. This makes Graphiti suitable for agents that need to reason about evolving facts over long time horizons.
Setup and Index Initialisation

Graphiti requires a running Neo4j instance for the graph store and an embedder for vector search. On first startup, call build_indices_and_constraints() to create the required Neo4j constraints and vector index. This call is idempotent — safe to include in every application startup.

# Install: pip install graphiti-core
# Requires: Neo4j (graph store) + OpenAI or compatible LLM + embedder

import asyncio
from graphiti_core import Graphiti
from graphiti_core.llm_client import OpenAIClient
from graphiti_core.embedder import OpenAIEmbedder

# Connect to Neo4j and configure LLM + embedder
graphiti = Graphiti(
    neo4j_uri="bolt://localhost:7687",
    neo4j_user="neo4j",
    neo4j_password="password",
    llm_client=OpenAIClient(api_key="..."),
    embedder=OpenAIEmbedder(api_key="..."),
)

async def main():
    # Build indices on first run (creates Neo4j constraints + vector index)
    await graphiti.build_indices_and_constraints()

asyncio.run(main())
Adding Episodes

An episode is any unit of information: a chat message, a document paragraph, a structured JSON fact, or a spoken transcript segment. add_episode() runs the full LLM extraction pipeline: entity detection, edge inference, contradiction resolution, and embedding. Pass reference_time to set the temporal anchor of the facts in that episode. Use group_id to scope all episodes to a specific user or session.

# add_episode() ingests one unit of information (a message, a document chunk,
# or a structured fact). Graphiti runs the LLM extraction pipeline in the
# background: entity detection, edge inference, contradiction resolution, embedding.

import asyncio
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from graphiti_core import Graphiti
from graphiti_core.nodes import EpisodeType

async def ingest_conversation(graphiti: Graphiti, session_id: str):
    messages = [
        ("Alice is the tech lead for the FastAPI project.",       "2024-11-01"),
        ("Bob was promoted to senior engineer on 2024-10-15.",    "2024-11-01"),
        ("The project budget is $80k and deadline is Dec 15.",    "2024-11-02"),
        ("Alice approved switching from Flask to FastAPI.",       "2024-11-03"),
    ]

    for content, date_str in messages:
        await graphiti.add_episode(
            name=f"{session_id}_{date_str}",
            episode_body=content,
            source=EpisodeType.message,
            source_description="project standup notes",
            reference_time=datetime.fromisoformat(date_str).replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc),
            group_id=session_id,      # scope to this session / user
        )

    print("Episodes ingested.")

asyncio.run(ingest_conversation(graphiti, group_id="project_x"))
Searching the Graph

search() uses a hybrid retrieval strategy: BM25 keyword matching, dense vector similarity over edge embeddings, and graph traversal from matched entity nodes. Results are EdgeResult objects — each carries the extracted fact string, the validity window, and the source episode text. Filter by group_ids to restrict search to a specific user or session, and filter by invalid_at=None to return only currently-valid facts.

# search() queries the graph using a hybrid of BM25 keyword, vector similarity,
# and graph traversal. It returns EdgeResult objects — each carries the fact text
# (edge name), its validity window (valid_at / invalid_at), and the source episode.

import asyncio
from graphiti_core import Graphiti

async def query(graphiti: Graphiti):
    results = await graphiti.search(
        query="who is the tech lead?",
        group_ids=["project_x"],   # restrict to this scope
        num_results=5,
    )

    for r in results:
        fact = r.fact            # extracted fact string, e.g. "Alice is tech lead"
        valid = r.valid_at       # when this fact became true
        invalid = r.invalid_at   # None = still current
        print(f"[{valid} → {invalid or 'now'}] {fact}")

    # Node search: find entity nodes by name or embedding similarity
    nodes = await graphiti.search_nodes(
        query="Alice",
        group_ids=["project_x"],
        num_results=3,
    )
    for n in nodes:
        print(f"Node: {n.name} ({n.entity_type})")

asyncio.run(query(graphiti))
Temporal Contradiction Handling

When a new episode introduces a fact that contradicts an existing edge for the same entity, Graphiti sets invalid_at on the old edge to the reference_time of the new episode. Both edges remain in the graph: the superseded edge is still queryable for audit and historical analysis, but search() by default surfaces the current one. This enables an agent to answer 'what is Bob's role now?' and 'what was Bob's role in October?' from the same graph.

# Graphiti automatically handles temporal contradictions.
# When a new episode contradicts a prior fact, the old edge is marked
# invalid_at = reference_time of the new episode. Both edges remain in the
# graph — the old one is queryable for audit; the new one is "current".

import asyncio
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from graphiti_core import Graphiti
from graphiti_core.nodes import EpisodeType

async def show_temporal(graphiti: Graphiti):
    group_id = "org_timeline"

    # Fact 1: Bob is a junior engineer (October)
    await graphiti.add_episode(
        name="oct_fact",
        episode_body="Bob is a junior engineer.",
        source=EpisodeType.message,
        reference_time=datetime(2024, 10, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
        group_id=group_id,
    )

    # Fact 2: Bob was promoted (November) — contradicts Fact 1
    await graphiti.add_episode(
        name="nov_fact",
        episode_body="Bob was promoted to senior engineer.",
        source=EpisodeType.message,
        reference_time=datetime(2024, 11, 15, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
        group_id=group_id,
    )

    # Query: returns ONLY the current fact (invalid_at=None)
    results = await graphiti.search("what is Bob's role?", group_ids=[group_id])
    for r in results:
        status = "CURRENT" if r.invalid_at is None else f"SUPERSEDED at {r.invalid_at}"
        print(f"[{status}] {r.fact}")

asyncio.run(show_temporal(graphiti))
Agent Integration Pattern

A standard agent loop with Graphiti: store each user turn as an episode, search for relevant current facts before each LLM call, inject the facts into the system prompt, then store the assistant reply as another episode. Filtering by invalid_at=None in the search ensures the agent only sees current facts, not superseded ones.

# Integrating Graphiti into an agent loop:
# 1. Store each user turn as an episode.
# 2. Search the graph before each LLM call to retrieve relevant current facts.
# 3. Inject ranked facts into the system prompt.

import asyncio
from graphiti_core import Graphiti
from graphiti_core.nodes import EpisodeType
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

oai = AsyncOpenAI(api_key="...")

async def agent_turn(graphiti: Graphiti, user_id: str, user_input: str) -> str:
    # Store the incoming user message
    await graphiti.add_episode(
        name=f"{user_id}_{datetime.now().isoformat()}",
        episode_body=user_input,
        source=EpisodeType.message,
        reference_time=datetime.now(timezone.utc),
        group_id=user_id,
    )

    # Retrieve up to 6 relevant current facts
    facts = await graphiti.search(user_input, group_ids=[user_id], num_results=6)
    fact_lines = [f"- {r.fact}" for r in facts if r.invalid_at is None]
    memory_block = "\n".join(fact_lines) if fact_lines else "(no relevant memory)"

    messages = [
        {"role": "system",
         "content": f"You are a helpful assistant.\n\nRelevant memory:\n{memory_block}"},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_input},
    ]

    resp = await oai.chat.completions.create(model="gpt-4o-mini", messages=messages)
    reply = resp.choices[0].message.content

    # Store the assistant reply as well
    await graphiti.add_episode(
        name=f"{user_id}_reply_{datetime.now().isoformat()}",
        episode_body=reply,
        source=EpisodeType.message,
        reference_time=datetime.now(timezone.utc),
        group_id=user_id,
    )
    return reply
Common Pitfalls
Async-only API
— All Graphiti methods are coroutines — you must use asyncio.run() or await them inside an async function. Calling them synchronously raises a RuntimeError.
build_indices_and_constraints() is required on first run
— Skip it and Neo4j won't have the vector index or uniqueness constraints needed for search. Call it once at startup; it is idempotent.
Extraction latency per episode

— Each add_episode() call makes multiple LLM requests (entity extraction, edge inference, contradiction check). For bulk ingestion, batch episodes and run them concurrently with asyncio.gather() rather than awaiting each one sequentially.
group_id is not optional in multi-tenant use
— All search() and add_episode() calls accept a group_ids filter. Without it, results from all users/sessions are mixed. Always pass a group_id equal to the user or session identifier.
Contradictions require overlapping entities

— Automatic contradiction resolution only fires when Graphiti detects that two edges share the same subject entity. If the same fact is expressed with different entity names across episodes (e.g. 'Bob' vs 'Robert'), no contradiction is detected.
Bulk Ingestion with Concurrent Episodes

Each add_episode() call makes several LLM requests — sequential ingestion of many documents is slow. Use asyncio.gather() to run multiple add_episode() calls concurrently. The extraction pipelines are independent, so there is no correctness risk in parallelising them. Tune the concurrency level based on the LLM provider's rate limits — five to ten concurrent calls is a reasonable starting point for OpenAI.

# Bulk ingest: gather add_episode() calls concurrently to amortize LLM latency.
# Each episode still runs the full extraction pipeline, but they run in parallel.

import asyncio
from graphiti_core.nodes import EpisodeType
from datetime import datetime, timezone

documents = [
    'Alice joined the team as tech lead in September 2024.',
    'The primary database is Postgres 16 with pgvector extension.',
    'Bob completed the API migration to FastAPI on October 20.',
    'The CI pipeline uses GitHub Actions and deploys to AWS ECS.',
    'The project deadline was extended to January 15 from December 15.',
]

async def bulk_ingest(graphiti, group_id: str):
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    tasks = [
        graphiti.add_episode(
            name=f'doc_{i}',
            episode_body=text,
            source=EpisodeType.text,
            reference_time=now,
            group_id=group_id,
        )
        for i, text in enumerate(documents)
    ]
    # Run up to 5 extractions in parallel — adjust based on LLM rate limits
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
    ok = sum(1 for r in results if not isinstance(r, Exception))
    print(f'Ingested {ok}/{len(documents)} episodes.')

asyncio.run(bulk_ingest(graphiti, group_id='project_x'))
Custom Entity Types

By default Graphiti uses generic entity types (Person, Organization, Location). For domain-specific agents, register Pydantic models as custom entity schemas at construction time. The LLM extractor will use your schema's field descriptions as extraction instructions, producing structured entity nodes (ServiceEntity with service_type and version, PersonEntity with role and team) instead of freeform labels. Custom entities improve both extraction precision and search relevance.

# Register custom entity types so Graphiti extracts domain-specific nodes.
# Without customisation the LLM uses generic 'Person', 'Organization' types.

from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from graphiti_core.nodes import EntityNode
from graphiti_core import Graphiti
from graphiti_core.llm_client import OpenAIClient
from graphiti_core.embedder import OpenAIEmbedder

# Define a custom entity schema
class ServiceEntity(BaseModel):
    name: str
    service_type: str = Field(description='Type: api, database, queue, cache')
    version: str | None = None
    deployed_at: str | None = None

class PersonEntity(BaseModel):
    name: str
    role: str = Field(description='Current job title or role')
    team: str | None = None

# Pass entity types at Graphiti construction
graphiti = Graphiti(
    neo4j_uri='bolt://localhost:7687',
    neo4j_user='neo4j',
    neo4j_password='password',
    llm_client=OpenAIClient(api_key='...'),
    embedder=OpenAIEmbedder(api_key='...'),
    entity_types=[ServiceEntity, PersonEntity],
)
# Now add_episode() will extract ServiceEntity and PersonEntity nodes
# instead of (or in addition to) generic types.
Summary
Graphiti builds a temporal knowledge graph from text episodes: entity nodes, typed edges (facts), and validity timestamps stored in Neo4j.
Each edge carries valid_at and invalid_at — when a new fact contradicts an old one, the old edge is marked superseded rather than deleted, preserving audit history.
search() combines BM25, vector similarity, and graph traversal; filter by invalid_at=None to retrieve only currently-valid facts.
Always pass group_id to scope episodes and searches to a specific user or session in multi-tenant deployments.
The API is fully async — wrap all calls in asyncio.run() or an async context; call build_indices_and_constraints() on startup.
